title: GDPR Consent Mechanism Audit (Lawful Basis Alignment)

compliance.assurance_rationale: GDPR Art.25(1) — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.

app: HTTP Event Collector (HEC), Splunk Add-on for Stream (Splunkbase 1809)
spl:
(index=web sourcetype="js_tag") earliest=-24h
| eval before_consent=if(consent_state="unknown" AND match(tag_category,"(?i)marketing|analytics"),1,0)
| stats sum(before_consent) as early_nonessential_tags by site_id tag_vendor
| where early_nonessential_tags > 100
| lookup gdpr_consent_policy.csv site_id OUTPUT required_banner_version
| join site_id [
    search index=consent earliest=-24h
    | stats latest(banner_version) as live_banner by site_id
]
| where live_banner != required_banner_version
| table site_id, tag_vendor, early_nonessential_tags, live_banner, required_banner_version
description: By design, consent collection must be granular and verifiable. This use case monitors CMP and web telemetry for consent banner versions, non-essential cookies fired before consent, and mismatches between claimed purposes in the CMP and tags observed in the browser stream.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for GDPR, UK GDPR compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes consent, web and sourcetypes js_tag. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Instrument sites to emit tag and consent state events; (2) define required banner versions per site in policy lookup; (3) alert marketing and privacy when non-essential tags load pre-consent; (4) block releases that downgrade banner version without approval; (5) align with UC-22.1.5/UC-22.1.16 for withdrawal enforcement. Indexes required: `index=consent`, `index=web`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=js_tag`.
visualization: Table (sites with violations), Single value (sites off-policy), Heatmap (violations by region), Bar chart (tag vendors). Key fields: `before_consent`, `early_nonessential_tags`, `live_banner`, `required_banner_version`, `site_id`, `tag_vendor`.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Consent version transitions** — During CMP configuration updates, brief periods may show consent records against outdated consent versions while the new configuration propagates to all touchpoints.

2. **Implied consent for contract performance** — Processing necessary for contract performance under Art.6(1)(b) does not require consent; the absence of consent records for these purposes is correct.

3. **Backend consent synchronisation** — Consent captured on one channel (web) may take time to propagate to all processing systems; check the consent propagation SLA.

4. **Pre-GDPR user base** — Long-standing users whose accounts predate the current consent mechanism may have equivalent consent captured under previous terms accepted at registration.

5. **Bot and crawler interactions** — Automated web crawlers and bots interacting with consent banners may generate consent events that are not associated with real data subjects.

**Operational suppression:** Maintain a lookup table (`gdpr_approved_exceptions.csv`) mapping known legitimate activities by process owner, approval reference, and expiry date. Filter the SPL results against this lookup to suppress documented exceptions and reduce alert fatigue. Review and rotate entries quarterly.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `web`, `consent` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `gdpr_consent_policy.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('gdpr_consent_policy.csv')`.
• **Search-head capacity**: Subsearch-based detections need `subsearch_maxtime` ≥ 60s and `subsearch_maxout` ≥ 50000 in `limits.conf`. For high-volume environments, tune `[search] max_searches_per_cpu` up from default.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.25(1)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that GDPR Art.25(1) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.46: GDPR Consent Mechanism Audit (Lawful Basis Alignment)." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `web`, `consent` under `js_tag`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Instrument sites to emit tag and consent state events. CIM-compliance tagging must be validated end-to-end: a downstream datamodel that does not accelerate because of a missing tag is a silent failure.

• **Hint 2:** define required banner versions per site in policy lookup. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** alert marketing and privacy when non-essential tags load pre-consent. CIM-compliance tagging must be validated end-to-end: a downstream datamodel that does not accelerate because of a missing tag is a silent failure.

• **Hint 4:** block releases that downgrade banner version without approval. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** align with UC-22.1.5/UC-22.1.16 for withdrawal enforcement. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "GDPR Consent Mechanism Audit (Lawful Basis Alignment)," the aggregation is the preference-management evidence — each row represents a data subject's current consent state, and drift from the authoritative preference store is the finding.

This search correlates `index=web` (sourcetype=`js_tag`) on the outer side with `index=consent` (sourcetype=`another`) on the inner side, joined on `site_id` across the last 24 hours. `type=inner` means the search only returns rows where `site_id` exists in both datasets. This is the correct choice when the detection requires presence in both sources — a left-join would include orphaned rows that cannot be evaluated against the compliance rule.

Join caveat: Splunk's `| join` subsearch is capped at `max_searches_per_cpu * subsearch_maxout` results (default: 50000). If the inner subsearch exceeds this, rows are silently dropped and the resulting report will under-count. Check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd log_level=WARN subsearch` for truncation warnings. For inner searches expected to return >10000 rows, rewrite as `| stats` with a larger base search and post-filter, or use `| tstats` if a CIM datamodel covers the data.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.1.46: GDPR Consent Mechanism Audit (Lawful Basis Alignment)]
cron_schedule = 0 1 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.1.46,reg=GDPR
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=web sourcetype=js_tag earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `before_consent`, `early_nonessential_tags`, `live_banner`, `required_banner_version`, `site_id`, `tag_vendor`. Run:

```spl
index=web sourcetype=js_tag earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table before_consent, early_nonessential_tags, live_banner, required_banner_version, site_id, tag_vendor
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `gdpr_consent_policy.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup gdpr_consent_policy.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `early_nonessential_tags`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(early_nonessential_tags) as p50 perc90(early_nonessential_tags) as p90 perc99(early_nonessential_tags) as p99 max(early_nonessential_tags) as hi
```

The current threshold `100` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.1.46: GDPR evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`sites with violations`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Single-value KPI (`sites off-policy`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'GDPR current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.

**Panel 3 — Heatmap (`violations by region`):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 4 — Distribution (`tag vendors`):** Shows the volumetric distribution across the primary dimension. Use `sort` so the worst offender is first — auditors focus on the tallest bar.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** The most likely cause is subsearch truncation (see Step 2). Inspect `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=searchprocessor log_level=WARN subsearch` for messages like 'Output result count exceeded 50000.'

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**GDPR-specific failure modes:**

• Lawful basis drift: a detection that worked when the processing activity had consent as its lawful basis becomes misleading when the business team silently changes the RoPA (Record of Processing Activities) entry to 'legitimate interest.' Every GDPR compliance search should reference the RoPA entry ID in its comments and re-review the detection when the RoPA is revised — at minimum annually.

• Data Subject Rights timers: Article 12(3) requires response to access/erasure/portability requests within one month. Detections that measure DSR fulfilment must use business-day clocks (not wall-clock 30 days) for regulators that enforce the distinction; the threshold value in the search should align with the local Supervisory Authority's interpretation, not the ISO 8601 literal.

• Cross-border transfer fragility: Schrems II invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield in 2020 and the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (2023) replaced it; any detection involving transfer monitoring must key off the current adequacy decision list, not a hardcoded country allowlist. Maintain `gdpr_adequacy_list.csv` and refresh when the EDPB updates the adequacy register.

• Breach notification 72-hour window (Art. 33): the clock starts at awareness, not at the forensically-confirmed moment of compromise. Detections must capture the first-awareness event (SOC alert acknowledgment, first ticket open, first email notification) — not just the incident-resolved timestamp — or the notification SLA metric will understate breaches that took 72h+1h to confirm.
grandmaExplanation: We by design, consent collection must be granular and verifiable so we can show we meet our privacy duties when people ask for proof.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Create a test processing activity tagged with lawful basis consent (Art.6(1)(a)) where the corresponding CMP consent record shows no valid consent captured for 20% of data subjects; verify the audit detects the consent gap.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Create a test processing activity with consent as lawful basis where all affected data subjects have valid, documented consent in the CMP with correct purpose scope; verify no consent gap alert fires.

evidence: Consent mechanism audit report showing lawful basis alignment per processing activity, consent capture rates, validity verification results, and gap remediation actions.
exclusions: Processing under lawful bases other than consent (contract, legal obligation, legitimate interest) where consent is not the relied-upon basis for that specific processing activity.
